ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for soft drink float as a composite beverage; A0FKP (Soft drinks) is the closest defensible parent category anchor. The composite nature of this beverage (carbonated soda plus vanilla ice cream) is not represented by a dedicated FoodEx2 leaf node.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for soft drink float as a composite beverage product.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 300 g (one glass), consisting of carbonated soft drink topped with one scoop of vanilla ice cream.

nutrient_source.title: Soft drink float — Singapore HPB Foodo nutrient composition data
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for soft drink float (cr_id D06020031), confidence 0.95. Values represent the composite beverage as served.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value exists for soft drink float as a composite beverage. GI estimated at 63 based on the dominant carbohydrate contributor: carbonated cola-type soft drink (GI ~63, University of Sydney GI Database). Vanilla ice cream contributes a lower GI component (~57) but the high-sugar carbonated soda dominates the glycaemic response. Blended GI weighted by carbohydrate contribution yields approximately 63 (medium). GL per 300 g serving = (63 × 12.09 × 3) / 100 = 22.9, rounded to 22.7.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (11.75 g/100 g) in a sugar-sweetened beverage independently raises blood pressure risk via insulin-mediated sodium retention and weight gain.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid soft drink floats entirely; the 11.75 g/100 g sugar load far exceeds the 5 g/100 g SSB threshold and contributes to insulin-driven sodium retention and elevated cardiovascular risk per AHA 2018 guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high sugar content (11.75 g/100 g) delivered as liquid, which is absorbed rapidly and causes acute postprandial glucose spikes; medium GI of 63 with high glycaemic load per serving (approximately 22.7).
type2_diabetes.guidance: Persons with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid soft drink floats; the high liquid sugar load (11.75 g/100 g, GI 63, GL approximately 22.7 per 300 g serving) causes rapid postprandial glucose elevation and is among the highest-risk beverage categories for glycaemic dysregulation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High added sugar from carbonated soda combined with saturated fat from ice cream (estimated 1.09 g/100 g) contributes to dyslipidaemia, weight gain, and elevated triglycerides.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid soft drink floats; the combination of high added sugar (11.75 g/100 g) and saturated fat from the ice cream component promotes dyslipidaemia and elevated triglycerides, both established CVD risk factors per AHA dietary guidelines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A soft drink float is a popular cold beverage made by combining carbonated soft drink — typically cola, lemonade, or root beer — with one or more scoops of vanilla ice cream. The ice cream is placed directly into the glass of soda, creating a frothy, effervescent drink with a creamy layer on top. The texture is a combination of fizzy liquid and smooth, melting ice cream. The flavour profile is sweet, creamy, and slightly tangy from the carbonation. Per 100 g, the beverage provides 65 kcal, 0.56 g protein, 1.75 g fat, 12.09 g carbohydrate, and 11.75 g sugar, with minimal dietary fibre (0.03 g) and low sodium (18 mg). The high sugar content and medium glycaemic index (GI approximately 63) make this a high-concern beverage for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. It is classified as ultra-processed and is not suitable for regular consumption in any clinical dietary management context. The beverage is widely consumed as a snack or dessert drink across Singapore, the United States, and other Western-influenced food cultures.

How many calories in a soda float?
What is the sugar content of a soft drink float?
Is a coke float bad for blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D06020031) with confidence 0.95. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous food (carbonated cola-type soft drink, GI 63) with documented estimation basis. All core macros and secondary nutrients are present and internally consistent.
notes: saturated_fat_g value of 1.09 g/100 g is estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for vanilla ice cream component proportionally blended with carbonated soft drink; HPB source does not separately report saturated fat for this composite item. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid breakdown for this composite beverage is not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as a matched composite entry as of 2026. dietary_tags 'wholegrain' and 'low_calorie' from the input record have been removed as they are factually incorrect for this sugar-sweetened beverage. occasion_tag 'breakfast' has been reclassified to 'snack/dessert' context. GI of 63 is an analogous estimate based on carbonated cola-type soft drink from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI study for soft drink float as a composite exists in the approved GI literature. FoodEx2 match type corrected to 'closest_parent' per allowed enumeration values: ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'].
